payload.text:
🧠 NEXUS 360
Sistema Operacional de Alta Performance Pessoal e Profissional
🎯 VISÃO GERAL DO PRODUTO
O Nexus 360 é um sistema inteligente que integra:
Tempo
Corpo
Dinheiro
Carreira
Decisões
Tudo conectado por IA adaptativa, que aprende com o usuário e orienta ações reais, não só exibe dados.
📌 Diferencial central:
O app não pergunta “o que você quer fazer?”, ele mostra “o que você deveria fazer agora”.
🧩 ESTRUTURA GERAL DE ABAS
Hoje (Dashboard Central)
Produtividade & Foco
Profissional (CRM & Vendas)
Treino & Corpo
Financeiro & Investimentos
Relatórios & Insights
(Opcional futuro) Planejamento & Metas
1️⃣ ABA “HOJE” – CENTRAL DE DECISÕES
Objetivo
Dar clareza imediata do dia e direcionar ação.
Componentes
🔹 Check-in rápido (30s):
Energia (0–10)
Humor
Qualidade do sono
Objetivo principal do dia
🔹 Agenda do dia (derivada da aba produtividade)
🔹 Alertas inteligentes
🔹 Insight da IA do dia
IA ATUANDO
Compara dias bons vs ruins
Sugere:
“Hoje, se você treinar antes das 10h, sua produtividade tende a ser maior.”
2️⃣ PRODUTIVIDADE & FOCO (CONTROLE HORA A HORA)
Estrutura
Linha do tempo de 24h
Registro manual ou automático
Categorias:
Trabalho
Estudo
Treino
Vendas
Lazer
Distração
Descanso
Funcionalidades Avançadas
🔔 Alerta de inatividade
⏱️ Botão “Estou fazendo agora”
📌 Classificação automática por IA (se o usuário errar)
IA ATUANDO
Detecta:
Perda de foco
Horários improdutivos
Gera relatórios como:
“Você rende 32% mais entre 9h e 11h.”
3️⃣ PROFISSIONAL – CRM + PERFORMANCE
CRM INTELIGENTE
Funis configuráveis
Lead
Contato
Proposta
Negociação
Fechado
Perdido
Cada lead possui:
Valor potencial
Origem
Probabilidade de fechamento
Próxima ação
Data de follow-up
IA ATUANDO NO CRM
Sugere:
Melhor horário de contato
Mensagens de follow-up
Analisa:
Gargalos do funil
Perdas recorrentes
📌 Insight exemplo:
“Você perde mais vendas por falta de follow-up do que por preço.”
PRODUTIVIDADE PROFISSIONAL
Tarefas ligadas a leads
Tempo gasto por cliente
ROI por atividade
4️⃣ TREINO & CORPO (UM DOS PONTOS MAIS FORTES)
ETAPA 1 – QUESTIONÁRIO INTELIGENTE
Sexo
Idade
Altura
Peso
Nível de treino
Limitações físicas
Objetivo:
Emagrecimento
Hipertrofia
Condicionamento
Saúde geral
ETAPA 2 – PLANO DE TREINO ADAPTATIVO
Divisão semanal automática
Progressão baseada em:
Frequência
Feedback pós-treino
Evolução corporal
📌 Nada de treino fixo. O treino evolui com você.
ETAPA 3 – EXECUÇÃO DOS EXERCÍCIOS
Diferencial forte:
🎥 Animação 3D do exercício
🔄 Loop do movimento
📍 Destaque dos músculos ativados
❌ Erros comuns
✔️ Dicas de execução
IA ATUANDO
Ajusta carga e volume
Sugere descanso
Relaciona:
Treino x Energia x Produtividade
5️⃣ FINANCEIRO & INVESTIMENTOS (ALTO VALOR)
CONTROLE FINANCEIRO
Receitas
Despesas
Categorias automáticas
Saldo diário, semanal e mensal
IA FINANCEIRA PESSOAL
Perfil financeiro automático
Renda média
Capacidade de poupança
Perfil de risco:
Conservador
Moderado
Agressivo
INVESTIMENTOS GUIADOS POR IA
Funcionalidades
Simulações:
“Se investir R$300/mês…”
Sugestão de alocação:
Renda fixa
Ações
ETFs
Análise de mercado:
Tendências
Setores em alta
Alertas de risco
📌 Importante (jurídico):
“Sugestões educacionais, não recomendação direta.”
IA ATUANDO
Cruza renda + gastos + metas
Diz:
“Você pode investir sem comprometer sua segurança financeira.”
6️⃣ RELATÓRIOS & INSIGHTS (ONDE O APP BRILHA)
Relatórios Visuais
Dia / Semana / Mês
Gráficos:
Uso do tempo
Performance física
Evolução financeira
Resultados profissionais
INSIGHTS AUTOMÁTICOS
Onde você está desperdiçando tempo
Onde está evoluindo
O que está travando seus resultados
📌 Exemplo:
“Você trabalha muito, mas direciona pouco esforço para atividades de alto retorno.”
7️⃣ IA CENTRAL – CÉREBRO DO SISTEMA
A IA do Nexus:
Aprende com hábitos
Compara padrões
Propõe ajustes semanais
Funções:
Mentor
Analista
Coach
Consultor financeiro
Personal trainer
📌 PRONTO PARA O GOOGLE AI STUDIO
Você agora pode pedir para a IA:
“Crie a arquitetura de dados, fluxos de usuário, entidades, modelos de IA e prompts para o app Nexus 360 conforme esta especificação.”

**Framing the Application's Vision**

I'm starting to define the core purpose of "Nexus 360" as a high-performance OS. The idea is to integrate AI (Gemini) with modules: Dashboard, Productivity, and Professional, to create a focused experience. Right now, I'm examining each module to refine its specific feature sets.


**Detailing the Technical Blueprint**

I've been drilling down into the technical specifics. I'm thinking of a React 18+ framework, using TypeScript, Tailwind CSS for styling, and `lucide-react` for icons. For charting, I'm leaning toward `recharts`. The AI component will be `@google/genai` with `gemini-3-flash-preview`. Keeping state management simple with React Context or local state, and I'll use client-side conditional rendering for navigation.


**Outlining Feature Integration**

I'm now detailing each module's features. Specifically, I'm considering a timeline view and focus timer in Productivity. For the Professional module (CRM), I'm exploring Kanban and list views, plus lead details. The Body module (workout) needs onboarding, plan display, and exercise views. Finance will focus on tracking and simulations. Reports will generate correlations with charts. I'm focusing on defining the AI's role within each module.


**Mapping Out Core Components**

I'm solidifying the fundamental components: Layout, Dashboard (Today view), Productivity (time tracking), Professional (CRM), Body (workout), Finance (money management), Reports (charts), and an AI modal for assistance.

I'm now implementing the high-level code structure. I'm choosing a state-based tab system instead of `react-router-dom`, as it feels snappier for the OS-like scope. Default dark mode will suit the high-performance aesthetic. I'll stick to specified AI models and use `localStorage` for state persistence.


**Implementing Specific Modules**

I'm now focusing on implementing each module in detail. I'm starting to define data structures with `types.ts`, including `Lead`, `Workout`, `Transaction`, and `DailyLog`. I'm creating service functions in `geminiService.ts` to generate insights, workout plans, and financial analyses. In the main `App.tsx` file, I'm setting up the core state management for active tabs. I'm also adding Tailwind and using lucide-react and recharts. Specifically, the CRM will start as a status-grouped list, while Workout will have a profile form that drives the user interface. Lastly, Finance will use a pie chart for allocation with an input list.


**Refining Implementation Details**

I've just been addressing specific implementation details to ensure a solid foundation. I'm double-checking imports of icons from `lucide-react` for efficiency, and I'm verifying responsiveness of charts with `ResponsiveContainer`. I'm also adding a System Instruction to all my AI calls, so the models respond like "Nexus 360 AI". I'm also adding a new feature that will show the user a single, recommended, action to take that day, which is a key goal of this project.

entries.description: App metadata definition
Root HTML file with Tailwind CSS and Font dependencies
TypeScript definitions for the application state and data models
Global constants and configuration
Service layer for interacting with Google Gemini API
Main dashboard component ("Today" tab)
CRM and Professional Tracking Component
Body and Workout component with AI Generation
Finance Component with Pie Chart and Advice
Root App component handling Navigation and State
Entry point for React 18

payload.text: Quero tudo em português

**Planning Localization Effort**

I'm currently focused on the localization plan for "Nexus 360" into Portuguese. My immediate concern is updating key files. I've identified `metadata.json` for description updates and `constants.ts` for navigation labels and potentially mock data. I'm prioritizing these for efficient implementation.


**Defining Translation Scope**

I've scoped the translation tasks more concretely now. It looks like I need to translate quite a few files: `services/geminiService.ts` for system instructions and error messages. Also `components/Dashboard.tsx`, `components/Professional.tsx`, `components/Body.tsx`, and `components/Finance.tsx` for all UI elements. Finally, `App.tsx` requires attention, focusing on navigation and placeholder text. I will leave `types.ts` alone for now, focusing on display text only. Also I've translated the description already!


**Implementing Translation Changes**

I'm currently focused on the translation updates for the UI strings. I've translated the navigation items in `constants.ts` to Portuguese. For mock data, I'll translate only display text like `nextAction`, leaving internal identifiers in English for type safety. The system instructions in `geminiService.ts` are now translated. I've also updated the `getDailyInsight` string and prompt.


**Refining Command Responses**

I'm now refining the responses for several commands. I've updated the fallback and error messages for the core task focus. For the `analyzeCRM` command, the prompt is updated to include revenue-unlocking suggestions. Also, I'm working to make the AI generate Portuguese content for the `generateWorkoutPlan` command, while keeping the keys English for code consistency. I will also be working on translating the prompt for `getInvestmentAdvice`.


**Adapting UI Text**

I'm currently updating the UI text in `components/Dashboard.tsx` to Portuguese. This includes translating various labels like "Morning Protocol," "System Energy Level," and mood/sleep states. I've also translated the "Primary Directive" and its placeholder text. I'm focusing on ensuring accuracy and a natural flow in the Portuguese translations.


**Translating Key Strings**

I'm translating various strings to Portuguese now. I have translated terms like "Bio-Energy," "Sleep Recovery," and several UI elements within `Dashboard.tsx` such as "Engage Focus Mode" and "System Alerts". I'm also addressing text from the `Professional.tsx` component, including labels like "Professional CRM" and "Pipeline Performance." My current focus is accurately translating these UI components.


**Localizing Body Component**

I'm now translating the UI elements within `components/Body.tsx`. The headers and labels are updated to Portuguese. This includes translating various phrases such as "Biometria e Metas," "Configure seu perfil...", "Peso (kg)," "Idade," and "Objetivo Primário". Also, I translated the options for the Primary Objective. I've focused on terms like "Hipertrofia" and "Saúde Geral".


**Finalizing Component Translations**

I'm translating various strings to Portuguese for the `Body.tsx` component, focusing on exercise-related terms such as "Séries," "Reps," and "Dica." I also updated the text for "Análise de Movimento IA," and a prompt related to exercise selection. Moving on to `Finance.tsx`, I've translated various financial terms like "Comando Financeiro," "Fluxo de Caixa," "Taxa de Queima," and "Capital Investível." I also translated the disclaimer about educational purposes. Finally, I translated the "Module under construction..." text in `App.tsx`.


**Adapting Type Translations**

I'm now implementing the UI translations and am checking the string unions in `types.ts`. I will keep the internal values of type strings in English to avoid breaking code logic, but will translate the UI labels. For example, in `Body.tsx`, I'll translate the workout goals. Also, I'll translate the lead statuses in `Professional.tsx`. Finally, I will save `mood` and `sleep` values from the check-in form in Portuguese now.


**Updating Language Scope**

I'm now refining translations based on the project requirements. I'm focusing on the `constants.ts` mock data, translating `nextAction` for leads and `category` for transactions, but keeping the internal `status` identifiers in English. I'm also updating prompts in `geminiService.ts` to request Portuguese responses, ensuring the generated content is also Portuguese.


**Mapping UI Values**

I'm translating the status display in `Professional.tsx`, using a mapping for UI display while retaining English keys. I'm focusing on the `lead.status` field and translating "proposal", "negotiation", "closed", "lost", "lead", and "contact." I'm doing the same for the workout goals within `Body.tsx`, translating the displayed options but keeping the underlying data values in English. I'm now ready to generate XML!

Atuando como um Engenheiro Frontend Sênior especializado em Localização e UX, vou traduzir completamente a aplicação **Nexus 360** para o Português (Brasil).

Vou adaptar não apenas os textos da interface, mas também os prompts da IA para garantir que as respostas geradas pelo Gemini sejam culturalmente relevantes e em português nativo.

Aqui estão as alterações detalhadas:

### 1. `metadata.json`
Atualização da descrição do aplicativo para português.

### 2. `constants.ts`
Tradução dos itens de navegação e dos dados de exemplo (Mock Data) para que a experiência inicial seja totalmente em português.

### 3. `services/geminiService.ts`
*   **Instrução de Sistema:** Reformulada para português para definir a "persona" da IA como o Nexus 360 em PT-BR.
*   **Prompts:** Todos os prompts enviados para o modelo foram traduzidos para garantir que o contexto e a saída (Insights, Planos de Treino, Conselhos Financeiros) sejam gerados em português.

### 4. Componentes (`Dashboard`, `Professional`, `Body`, `Finance`, `App`)

payload.text:
- Na aba 1, eu quero mudar totalmente, eu quero formular essa ideia na realidade. Eu quero que, em vez de ela ser do jeito que ela está, eu quero um modo, como se fosse a página inicial do Gemini ou do ChatGPT, que o cliente ele consegue ali tirar as dúvidas, com a inteligência artificial, com qualquer dúvida que ele venha a ter. E também que seja um lançador de, de, de comandos. Um lançador de comandos. Por exemplo, ele pode escrever diretamente ali que ele gastou R$ 15 no café da manhã, e isso já vai ser lançado automaticamente nas finanças. Ou ele pode falar que fez um treino de braços com 15 repetições, e isso já ser lançado automaticamente na sua aba correta.
- Na aba 2, eu quero um checklist de todas as tarefas de toda a parte produtiva.

**Reimagining the Dashboard**

I'm now diving deep into the user request, specifically focusing on the initial tab. My aim is a complete redesign. The current iteration is a static form leading into a dashboard view, but my goal is to innovate beyond this. I see great potential.


**Defining the Chat Interface**

I've made a conceptual leap. Now, I'm envisioning the first tab as a dynamic chat interface – a "Nexus AI." The key functionality includes general Q&A and a "Command Launcher" that interprets natural language commands. For the second tab, the "Productivity" section, I'm working to expand the current placeholder into a comprehensive checklist to capture productive tasks.


**Establishing AI Execution Logic**

I'm now grappling with the nuance of AI-driven actions versus simple conversation. I'm focusing on delineating when a command should be executed versus just a response. I'm defining tools, like `addTransaction` and `logWorkout`, for Gemini. The frontend must be ready for "state changes" when the AI calls a tool, mocking database updates initially. The chat interface is now the central point of control. I'm lifting state management to `App.tsx` and passing setters down, given the project's structure. I've also moved to the checklist on the "Productivity" Tab, defining the UI, Data Structure, and logic needed to implement the requested productivity tools. I am now working through code changes and defining the messages type.


**Developing Tool Interactions**

I'm now integrating AI tool usage into the chat interface. I need to develop a `chatWithNexus` function to manage the logic. This function will support tools like `logExpense`, `logWorkout`, and `addTask`. The prompt is updated to be a "Commander" persona. I'm restructuring `App.tsx` as the central state hub, handling data for transactions, leads, tasks, and workout logs, passing setters to child components. I am planning to move away from hardcoded mock data. Finally, I'm working to create the `handleCommand` logic.


**Implementing the Tab Logic**

I'm now completely restructuring the tab components. I'm replacing the Dashboard tab's logic with the chat interface, which will be scrollable with an input area. I am also integrating the new `Productivity.tsx` component to handle the checklist. Furthermore, I'm updating `Finance.tsx` and `Body.tsx` to handle props, as well as updating the Body component to show recent activities in response to chat input. I'm focusing on refining the tool use implementation using Gemini's `tools` configuration to simplify the backend logic. I plan to define `functionDeclarations` for `addTransaction`.


**Structuring the Chat Logic**

I'm solidifying the chat interface's core functionality. I'm building out the `chatWithNexus` function to manage the conversation flow. The function will use a new Gemini Service and manage the necessary state updates. The Gemini service will require a "sendMessage" function that takes history and tools. The App will manage the overall state: `transactions`, `tasks`, and `workoutLogs`. The Chat Component will maintain the `messages` state. The logic involves handling user input, making API calls, and displaying the AI's responses, including handling function calls.


**Defining UI Elements**

Now, I'm concentrating on the UI. I'm focusing on the overall design and making sure it looks modern, especially the chat bubbles and dark mode. I'm also ensuring the Gemini integration works with function calls. I'm carefully reviewing the structure of the `@google/genai` library.


**Organizing Component Logic**

I'm now recognizing that `App.tsx` may become unwieldy, despite being the cleanest way to share state here. I'm focusing on replacing Tab 1 with the chat interface by modifying `components/Dashboard.tsx` or, if cleaner, creating and importing `components/ChatHome.tsx`. I'm also preparing to build `components/Productivity.tsx` and ensure its proper integration, as it seems to be missing from the original structure.


**Refining Tab Integration**

I'm now integrating the Productivity component and updating Finance and Body to display data from the chat. I'm focusing on lifting state and making the data flow correctly. The Gemini service will get updated. I'm working to implement a robust tool execution system, updating the message history, including the tools. I plan to handle the message type, the new workout and transaction log data. I'll structure the component, then build the service.

Aqui está a especificação e as alterações para transformar o Nexus 360 conforme solicitado.

### Especificação das Alterações

**1. Transformação da Aba 1 (Dashboard) -> Interface de Chat "Nexus Command"**
*   **Conceito:** Uma interface estilo ChatGPT/Gemini que atua como o cérebro central.
*   **Funcionalidade de Conversa:** O usuário pode tirar dúvidas gerais (ex: "Como melhorar meu sono?", "Dicas de investimento").
*   **Lançador de Comandos (Function Calling):** A IA detectará intenções específicas para executar ações no app.
    *   *Finanças:* "Gastei 15 reais no café" -> Adiciona transação na Aba Financeira.
    *   *Corpo:* "Treino de braços, 15 repetições" -> Adiciona log na Aba Corpo.
    *   *Produtividade:* "Lembrar de ligar para o cliente amanhã" -> Adiciona tarefa na Aba Foco.
*   **Visual:** Área de chat com histórico, bolhas de mensagem diferenciadas (Usuário vs IA), e barra de entrada fixa na parte inferior. Feedback visual quando um comando é executado.

**2. Transformação da Aba 2 (Foco/Produtividade) -> Checklist de Tarefas**
*   **Conceito:** Um gerenciador de tarefas simples e direto.
*   **Funcionalidade:** Listar tarefas, adicionar novas, marcar como concluídas (checkbox), excluir.
*   **Integração:** As tarefas adicionadas pelo Chat na Aba 1 aparecerão aqui.
*   **Visual:** Lista limpa com checkboxes estilizados, separação visual entre tarefas pendentes e concluídas.

**3. Atualizações de Estado (App.tsx e Componentes)**
*   **App.tsx:** O estado dos dados (Transações, Logs de Treino, Tarefas) será movido para o componente pai (`App`) para que o Chat possa modificá-los e as outras abas (Finanças, Corpo, Foco) possam exibi-los.
*   **Finance.tsx & Body.tsx:** Serão atualizados para receber dados via `props` em vez de usar dados estáticos, permitindo que as atualizações do chat sejam refletidas em tempo real.

---

### Código

payload.text:
Quero que você melhore toda a parte dois da produtividade, tá? Me dê ideias do que pode ser melhor, pode implementar lá. Os horários, vai, das 5 da manhã até a meia-noite. Enfim, se quiser colocar da meia-noite até a meia-noite, acho que é até melhor, se for feito algo na madrugada, ou deixar ali o período de sono. Quero que essa parte da produtividade seja muito assertiva, com checklists. Nessa parte da produtividade, todo dia, na parte da manhã, quero que tenha uma aba para você lançar quais são as tarefas, ou até mesmo da semana para você lançar suas tarefas. E dentro disso, tem um checklist ali para colocar o que foi feito e o que não foi feito.

Além disso, gostaria de personalizar com lembretes e, se for possível, colocar como se fosse um Xp de bonificação, toda vez que um checklist for completo, sabe? Uma mensagemzinha ali: "Parabéns, você atingiu o objetivo."

Além disso, eu quero melhorar toda a parte do sistema. Tá bom? Então me dê ideias para que isso aconteça e o que já foi possível modificar para ficar melhor, pode fazer. 

Quero que você inclua uma aba de relatórios que seja a última. Essa precisa ser a última.

De relatórios com gráficos, mostrando qual foi a produtividade do dia, da semana e do mês. Por exemplo, hoje, você, se a pessoa não almoçou nada, lá vai constar que o dia dela não foi produtivo.

Precisamos dos lançamentos para passar essa informação para o nosso cliente. Por exemplo, se ele fez todos os lançamentos lá, ele estudou duas horas, ele trabalhou cinco, seis horas, ele almoçou, ele foi para a academia, o dia dele foi super produtivo. Então, a gente precisa expressar isso para ele.

Este relatório, ele precisa mostrar quantas horas ele ficou acordado e quantas horas ali ele conseguiu ter uma produtividade assertiva. Isso daí é essencial para, no final da semana, no final do mês, a gente ter números reais do que foi feito e do que pode ser melhorado.

Inclusive, tem uma aba ali de quanto tempo ficou só mexendo no telefone, de quanto tempo ficou sem fazer nada. Eu quero realmente ter o máximo de produtividade. Esse relatório, ele precisa nos passar o que pode ser melhorado. Tudo o que o, se o seu tempo está sendo bem aproveitado ou não.

**Focusing on Productivity Enhancements**

I'm zeroing in on the "Productivity" tab. The timeline view feels like a good first step for that area. It should provide a solid base to build upon. I'm imagining it will be a cornerstone of the feature. Let me know what you think.


**Revising Reporting and Data Structures**

I'm now diving into the requirements for the "Reports" tab and thinking about data structure updates. The reports must be the final tab. I envision visual graphs with daily, weekly, and monthly data, including a productivity score. I'm planning to make some additions to the existing `Task` type, specifically `startTime`, `endTime`, `category`, and `xpValue`, and a `DailyLog` type to aggregate productivity.


**Integrating Task and Activity Models**

I've decided to consolidate the task and activity models, incorporating duration and type directly into a unified `Task` or `Activity` model. This approach streamlines data management. I also need to make the state of `tasks` accessible to both the Productivity and Reports components. For added engagement, I'm integrating a user "XP" or "Level" state for gamification purposes.


**Analyzing Time Allocation Features**

I'm now thinking about how to integrate Gemini for time-tracking insights. Specifically, I'm working to expand chat tool capabilities to parse various time categories like 'wasted'. I envision a bar chart visualizing weekly trends. Then, dynamic insight text generation is next on my to-do list based on data analysis.


**Reimagining Timeline Display**

I've revised the timeline approach; rendering a full 24-hour block seems clunky. Instead, I'm leaning towards a more concise, chronologically sorted list, allowing for visual filling of activity slots. The focus is on showing awake hours and scheduled items, with gaps as visual opportunities. I'm also thinking through a simple XP system, where deep work and task completion will be rewarded. Finally, I'm adding an input for logging distractions, specifically 'phone time'.

**Drafting the Code**

Now, I'm focusing on implementation specifics. I'm planning to use `Trophy`, `AlertCircle`, `Calendar`, `Clock`, and `Smartphone` icons. For reports, I'm categorizing time as Productive (Work, Study, Workout, Self-Improvement), Neutral (Chores, Transit), and Unproductive (Social Media, TV, Doing Nothing), and calculating productivity as (Productive Hours / (Total Awake Hours - Neutral)) * 100.


**Developing Core Features**

I'm now focusing on specific file updates. I'm adding a "Plan Day" modal and task checklist to `Productivity.tsx`, with visual feedback on task completion. For the `Reports.tsx` component, I'm aggregating task durations by category. The `types.ts`, `constants.ts`, `Productivity.tsx`, and `Reports.tsx` files are being updated to support these changes and provide mock data for initial testing. I'm also ensuring the correct props are being passed down and `handleCommand` supports the new structure.


**Defining Key Calculations**

I'm now focusing on calculations for the Reports component. Specifically, I'm working on calculating "awake time," either using a standard wake-up time or deriving it from the first and last activities. I'm defaulting to 16 awake hours if no activity is tracked. I'm also treating "wasted time" as a completed task with a "wasted" category, which will be logged via `handleCommand` in `App.tsx`. I will adjust `Reports.tsx` to handle the `wasted` category from `handleCommand`.


**Adjusting Awake Time Logic**

I'm now implementing the calculation for awake time, specifically handling scenarios where no activity data exists. In those cases, I'm defaulting to 16 awake hours. I've also incorporated the ability to show a Day/Week toggle within the Productivity component, as the user mentioned the need to launch tasks for the week.

payload.text:
A primeira aba, preciso que você tire esse protocolo matinal. Vamos esquecer essa ideia de protocolo matinal. Eu preciso que você faça uma aba como se fosse muito parecido com o Gemini e muito parecido com o chat GPT, onde o cliente ele tem acesso à inteligência artificial e ele pode fazer perguntas do dia a dia dele, sobre qualquer questão da vida, igualzinho, muito semelhante ao chat GPT e ao Gemini.

Além disso, eu preciso que ele seja um controlador geral. Então, se o nosso cliente ele quiser fazer os lançamentos a partir dessa primeira aba, que isso seja possibilitado. Por exemplo, se ele em vez de ir lançar manualmente o treino dele hoje, ele pode lançar diretamente por essa aba e já se refletir na aba específica de treino ou de finanças. Ele pode fazer um lançamento de quanto ele gastou de almoço, ou de café da manhã, ou de janta, ou até mesmo se ele recebeu uma caixinha durante o trabalho, que será uma receita, ou que hoje ele recebeu o pagamento dele, e tudo isso na primeira aba.

Eu quero algo muito personalizado, muito bacana e que seja muito bonito.

**Reimagining the Dashboard**

I'm now zeroing in on the core goal: transforming the initial dashboard into something akin to a sophisticated AI chat interface. "Morning Protocol" is removed. My focus is now on mapping the elements of the existing tab to the target functionality and ensuring a seamless transition.


**Defining the UI Elements**

I'm solidifying the necessary components. The plan now centers on a general AI chat, a "General Controller" for natural language data input (workouts, finances, etc.), and auto-routing to the corresponding tabs. The aesthetic goal is high personalization, with a "very cool" and "very beautiful" visual approach.


**Expanding Command Handling**

I'm now focused on refining the command handling. The existing `handleCommand` in `App.tsx` already covers key actions, and I'll extend `logActivity` to encompass meals and general tasks. In `services/geminiService.ts`, the `SYSTEM_INSTRUCTION` prompt will be updated to portray the "General Controller" as a more approachable assistant. I need to keep the `CheckIn` types if Reports uses them.


**Adjusting Data Structures**

I'm evaluating whether to keep sleep data in `App.tsx` or hide it in reports to avoid code breakage. I'm leaning toward preserving the type but removing the UI. I've also drafted the mental model for the interface: a minimalist design with a "Nexus AI" header. The main area will show a hero section for new users, transitioning to a chat bubble interface as they start a conversation. A floating bottom bar will house the input field.


**Implementing the Chat Interface**

I'm now in the process of rebuilding the dashboard as an AI chatbot interface, focusing on key file modifications. The `Dashboard.tsx` component is undergoing a major rewrite, with `CheckIn` props removed and a dark theme with glassmorphism effects. I'm adding "Suggestion Cards" for the empty state and styling the input bar as a capsule. The `App.tsx` file is being updated to remove `hasCheckedInToday` logic, and I'm updating `renderContent` to pass only `onCommand` to the Dashboard. Because Reports uses `checkIns`, I'm adding `logSleep` functionality and corresponding updates in `geminiService.ts` and `App.tsx` so users can still track sleep. The plan is an iterative refinement of the code.


**Refining Interface Aesthetics**

I'm now incorporating `lucide-react` icons and Tailwind gradients to enhance the visual appeal of the interface. I'm focusing on "Sparkles" and "ArrowUp" icons, and implementing `bg-gradient-to-r` classes for gradients. Also, I realized I needed a method for meals, so the final choice will be a `logMeal` tool.


**Defining Interaction Guidelines**

I'm now making explicit rules for data routing based on user input. I'll automatically route financial requests, such as lunch expenses or tips, to the finance module. It's imperative that the sleep tool is added back for the Reports tab to function. I am now working on XML generation.

Aqui estão as alterações para transformar a primeira aba em uma interface de Chat AI "General Controller" (estilo Gemini/ChatGPT), removendo o protocolo matinal e adicionando controle total via chat (incluindo sono para os relatórios).

### Resumo das Mudanças

1.  **`services/geminiService.ts`**:
    *   Atualizei as instruções do sistema para ser mais conversacional e menos robótico.
    *   Adicionei a ferramenta `logSleep` para permitir lançar horas de sono pelo chat (já que removemos o formulário manual).
2.  **`components/Dashboard.tsx`**:
    *   **Remoção Total do Protocolo Matinal.**
    *   **Novo Design**: Interface limpa, com "Sugestões de Comandos" quando o chat está vazio (estilo ChatGPT).
    *   **UX**: Barra de input flutuante, moderna e centralizada.
3.  **`App.tsx`**:
    *   Removi a lógica de bloqueio de tela (`hasCheckedInToday`).
    *   Adicionei o tratamento do comando `logSleep` para alimentar o gráfico de sono na aba de Relatórios.

payload.text:
- Everything that is mentioned on the "Chronogram" tab, which is the second tab, is also reflected in the "Reports" section.
- Therefore, in the "Reports" section, there should be a tab showing completed tasks.
- If I schedule a meeting on the "Chronogram" tab, I need that meeting to be reflected in the reports within the "Reports" tab.
- If I put lunch on the chronogram, I need the lunch to be reflected in the "Reports" tab.
- If I put a haircut, I need the haircut to be reported in the "Reports" tab.
- All subsequent information.

**Considering Report Integration**

I'm focusing on integrating Chronogram data into the Reports section. The primary concern is ensuring complete and accurate reflection of scheduled and logged activities. I'm currently exploring the data flow, identifying key points for synchronization to avoid discrepancies, which is the biggest challenge to the design.

The current report calculations in `Reports.tsx` rely on specific task categories; I need to determine the best approach for integrating these activities into the existing time distribution pie chart and potentially introducing new report sections. The key is finding the right balance between detail and clarity.

The plan is to include all task categories in both views, refining the design with both statistical charts and textual listings.


**Integrating Detailed Tasks Display**

I'm now integrating all task categories within `Reports.tsx`, expanding from "Activity History Tabs". I'm adding a tab-based navigation using `useState` for 'overview' and 'tasks' views. I'm focusing on ensuring accurate calculations and display of totals for all categories, including "routine" and "leisure," and updating the `PieChart` data accordingly. I'm building the "tasks" list for display when the relevant tab is selected.


**Implementing Statistic Charts**

I'm now integrating the statistic calculation with the charts. I'm focusing on the `PieChart` to display time distribution across all task categories. **Updating Time Distribution**

I've updated the time distribution chart to include "Wasted" time, along with the existing categories. The `timeDistData` array now reflects this addition. I am also checking that the existing logic for finance and sleep is still present, and unaffected.


**Refining List View Implementation**

I'm now focusing on the "Tasks" list within the "Reports" section, ensuring it accurately displays all tasks with their status. I'm prioritizing clarity, designing table columns for Status (Icon), Activity (Title), Category (Badge), Date/Time, and Duration. I plan to use the `completed` boolean to visually distinguish completed tasks within the list, improving the display of scheduled versus executed activities in reports.


**Sorting Task Data**

I'm now sorting the tasks list by date in descending order to improve the organization of the "Tasks" list. I'm focusing on the implementation details and making a final verification of the `components/Reports.tsx` file to ensure all components and the data flow work correctly.


**Analyzing Financial Statistics**

I'm now integrating the calculation of financial statistics, focusing on daily and weekly income and expenses within `Reports.tsx`. I'm calculating and displaying today's and total financial data, along with average sleep hours. I am also planning to implement weekly financial stats. The main focus is to provide a concise financial overview for the user.



**Refining Chart Display**

I'm currently focused on the `PieChart` display, ensuring that it is responsive and visually appealing. I'm verifying the data integration for accurate time distribution. I have checked for consistent coloring and labeling, as well as the accurate portrayal of time values within the `timeDistData` array.


**Enhancing Chart Colors**

I'm now refining the color scheme in the charts to improve visual clarity and consistency. I am ensuring that colors are well-defined for each category and that they align with the rest of the application's design. I'm focusing on readability and accessibility to enhance the overall user experience. I am verifying consistent color mapping.

```

I'm now integrating the updated component structure into `Reports.tsx`, ensuring proper data flow and responsiveness. I'm focusing on the `PieChart` to display all relevant categories. I'm refining the data processing logic to ensure accurate representation of time distribution, and verifying the new icons. I'm prioritizing accuracy and visual appeal.

I've decided on the color coding: Work = `#3b82f6`, Study = `#8b5cf6`, Workout = `#10b981`, Routine = `#f59e0b`, Leisure = `#ec4899`, and Wasted = `#ef4444`.

**Finalizing Chart Implementation**

I'm verifying the visualization in the "Overview" tab. The bar chart appears in a new row with the data being accurately displayed. I've included the desired color coding. I now must ensure it functions correctly.


**Coding the Chart Display**

import React, { useState } from 'react'; import { Lead } from '../types'; import { MOCK_LEADS } from '../constants'; import { analyzeCRM } from '../services/geminiService'; import { TrendingUp, Users, DollarSign, Brain, MoreHorizontal, Plus, X, Save, MapPin, Instagram, Phone, Building2, User } from 'lucide-react'; export const Professional: React.FC = () => { const [leads, setLeads] = useState(MOCK_LEADS as any); const [aiAnalysis, setAiAnalysis] = useState(""); const [analyzing, setAnalyzing] = useState(false); const [showForm, setShowForm] = useState(false); // Form State const [formData, setFormData] = useState>({ name: '', company: '', cpfCnpj: '', instagram: '', phone: '', state: '', location: '', value: 0, status: 'lead', probability: 10, nextAction: 'Iniciar contato' }); const handleAnalyze = async () => { setAnalyzing(true); const result = await analyzeCRM(leads); setAiAnalysis(result); setAnalyzing(false); }; const handleSaveClient = (e: React.FormEvent) => { e.preventDefault(); if (!formData.name) return; const newClient: Lead = { id: Date.now().toString(), name: formData.name || 'Cliente Sem Nome', company: formData.company || 'N/A', status: (formData.status as any) || 'lead', value: Number(formData.value) || 0, probability: Number(formData.probability) || 0, lastContact: new Date().toISOString().split('T')[0], nextAction: formData.nextAction || 'Definir próximos passos', cpfCnpj: formData.cpfCnpj, instagram: formData.instagram, phone: formData.phone, state: formData.state, location: formData.location }; setLeads([...leads, newClient]); setShowForm(false); // Reset form setFormData({ name: '', company: '', cpfCnpj: '', instagram: '', phone: '', state: '', location: '', value: 0, status: 'lead', probability: 10, nextAction: 'Iniciar contato' }); }; const getStatusColor = (status: string) => { switch (status) { case 'proposal': return 'bg-purple-500/20 text-purple-400 border-purple-500/30'; case 'negotiation': return 'bg-amber-500/20 text-amber-400 border-amber-500/30'; case 'closed': return 'bg-emerald-500/20 text-emerald-400 border-emerald-500/30'; case 'lost': return 'bg-red-500/20 text-red-400 border-red-500/30'; default: return 'bg-blue-500/20 text-blue-400 border-blue-500/30'; case 'lead': return 'Lead'; case 'contact': return 'Contato'; default: return status; } } const totalPipeline = leads.reduce((acc, lead) => acc + lead.value, 0); const weightedPipeline = leads.reduce((acc, lead) => acc + (lead.value * (lead.probability / 100)), 0); payload.text: Para o CRM, precisamos criar uma separação fazendo um funil de vendas. Extremamente conversível para eu conseguir colocar cada cliente em sua fase de atendimento. Devo conseguir mudar os clientes de fase no funil só arrastando eles para aba desejada.

**Initiating Funnel Visualization**

I'm now diving into the specific request, starting with a crucial analysis of the CRM's requirements. My focus is on creating a Sales Funnel within the CRM, visualized using a Kanban-style approach. I'm prioritizing the separation of distinct phases, like lead, contact, etc. to align the solution with the project's goal.


**Implementing Drag and Drop**

I'm now fully focused on implementing drag-and-drop functionality, the core of the Kanban view. Considering that I must adhere to the existing framework's limitations, I've opted for native HTML5 drag-and-drop. I'm leveraging `draggable`, `onDragStart`, `onDragOver`, and `onDrop` attributes for a lightweight and dependency-free solution. The goal is to facilitate seamless client movement between phases.


**Structuring UI Components**

I'm now outlining the UI components for the Kanban board. The design will be a horizontal, scrollable layout with columns for each sales stage, and cards representing leads within them. I'll maintain a dark, slate-gray theme to match the app's overall style, and I'm sticking with the defined `status` types. I need to make the toggle for "List View" to "Funnel View."


**Drafting Drag-and-Drop Logic**

Now, I'm fleshing out the drag-and-drop mechanics. When a card is moved between columns, I need the state to update instantly. I'm focusing on the core card content: Name, Company, Value, Probability bar, and Next Action. My implementation plan involves updating `Professional.tsx`, with a state for `viewMode`, a toggle button, and drag-and-drop event handlers. I will render the Kanban board conditionally on the `viewMode`.



**Enhancing Visual Feedback**

I'm currently focused on the UI's visual cues. I'm introducing a color gradient for column headers, reflecting progress, and giving cards a tangible feel with borders and shadows. I'll also display the total value within each column. This change should make the funnel more intuitive at a glance. I am now incorporating the Kanban/Funnel view within `components/Professional.tsx`.


**Integrating Functional Refinements**

I've just added the `updateStatus` function to handle lead updates within the Kanban board, and my rendering loop now correctly iterates through columns, filtering and displaying cards dynamically. I have integrated all the defined stages, and translated the display labels for these into Portuguese: "Novos Leads," "Em Contato," "Proposta Enviada," "Em Negociação," "Fechado / Ganho," and "Perdido." Finally, I am polishing the responsiveness of the drag-and-drop interactions to guarantee smooth operation. I am currently adding the `overflow` setting to deal with too many columns.


**Generating XML Representation**

I'm now generating the XML, now adding `overflow-x: auto` to the container for improved handling of a large number of columns.

Para implementar um Funil de Vendas (Kanban) com funcionalidade de "arrastar e soltar" (Drag and Drop), farei as seguintes alterações no arquivo `components/Professional.tsx`:

1.  **Novo Modo de Visualização**: Adicionarei um botão para alternar entre a visão de "Lista" (tabela atual) e "Funil" (novo Kanban).
2.  **Lógica de Drag & Drop**: Utilizarei a API nativa do HTML5 para permitir arrastar os cartões dos clientes entre as colunas.
3.  **Visual do Funil**: Criarei colunas visuais para cada etapa (`Lead`, `Contato`, `Proposta`, `Negociação`, `Fechado`, `Perdido`) com totalizadores de valor por fase.

payload.text: Agora, preciso que você crie mais uma parte no funil de vendas, considerado o follow-up antes dos perdidos.

**Adding the Follow-up Stage**

I'm zeroing in on the core of the request. The objective is clear: incorporate a "Follow-up" stage into the Sales Funnel, strategically placing it between the "Fechado" and "Perdido" stages within the CRM system.


**Incorporating Follow-Up Logic**

I'm making progress. I've pinpointed the necessary changes in `types.ts` and `components/Professional.tsx`. The `Lead` interface in `types.ts` will get the `'followup'` status, and the color and display text in `Professional.tsx` are updated. - E aí, para a gente ter um relatório dentro da aba de CRM de onde são os clientes, para a gente poder também ter a noção de onde está sendo a nossa boa atuação e onde a gente não está tendo um bom desempenho, isso de acordo com os estados e o mapa.

**Comprehending the Objective**

Okay, so I'm now clear: the goal is to build a "Clients by Map" visualization within the CRM's "Professional" tab. This involves taking existing client address data and plotting it on a map interface. That's the core functionality I need to develop. I'm focusing on the integration of location data.


**Refining the Approach**

I'm now zeroing in on the best method. The initial goal is to provide geographical performance reports, so "Clients by Map" will be key. My current thinking leans towards a combination of mock/static coordinates, especially for the frontend prototype. It would still allow visualization of a map. However, State Aggregation is also on the cards, and the mock coordinates could include some state specific coordinates, too.


**Defining the Visualization Strategy**

I'm now solidifying my map visualization strategy. I've re-evaluated using `react-leaflet` via `esm.sh` as the primary approach, because I found it works nicely. I will also incorporate Leaflet CSS. As a fallback, I'll simulate a "Pin on Map" or implement state-based mapping, to ensure I can demonstrate map integration. If geocoding complexities arise, manual coordinate entry or "mock" geocoding will be included to demonstrate the concept.


**Outlining the Implementation**

I'm now detailing the implementation steps. I will update `index.html` with Leaflet CSS, and add address-related fields to the `Lead` interface. Then, I'll integrate `react-leaflet` components into `Professional.tsx`, add a geocoding helper (potentially using Nominatim, or mock data), and include a map component. The "New Client" form will also be updated to include address fields.

\n\n

**Finalizing Leaflet Setup**

I've outlined how to finalize the Leaflet integration using `esm.sh`. I need to handle `MapContainer`, `TileLayer`, `Marker`, `Popup` from `react-leaflet`. I will need `leaflet` too. I need to handle any "Window" object checks required for Leaflet rendering in this React environment, but the standard Client-Side rendering is fine.


**Establishing Geocoding and Forms**

My focus is now on integrating the geocoding service and updating the client form. I'm building a function to retrieve coordinates using Nominatim. The form will capture full addresses. I'll need to update the `types.ts` to include address, zip code, number, latitude, and longitude. Finally, I'll update the `Professional.tsx` to handle the new fields and geocoding trigger upon saving client data.


**Incorporating Visual Enhancements**

I'm now integrating the map section's visual aspects. I'll add a `viewMode` for potential future enhancements, perhaps at the bottom as suggested. I'll also add dependencies in `index.html` via `esm.sh` instead of adding to a nested `src/` directory. I'm also refining the geocoding integration: I'll trigger geocoding on the "Save" action for now, and implement "Search Address" button in the future. Finally, I'll display client markers on the map, color-coded by their status, to create a basic "Map Report".


**Integrating Leaflet with React**

I'm now focused on the practical implementation using `react-leaflet`. I'll update the `index.html` file to incorporate the necessary Leaflet and `react-leaflet` dependencies using the import map to ensure stability. I'll need to specify these exact versions for now. Then, I will update types, and integrate the map and forms in the Professional tab. Finally, I will integrate the "clients by state" summary.


**Detailing Implementation Steps**

I'm now detailing the implementation steps for `Professional.tsx`. I'll first add the required `react-leaflet` components such as `MapContainer`, `TileLayer`, `Marker`, and `Popup`. I'll then create a `getCoordinates` function that uses `fetch`. This will be used in `handleSaveClient` to retrieve coordinates, updating the client's information. I will also make the Geocoder construct a query string like "Rua Clara Nunes 94B, São Paulo, Brazil" during geocoding. I will handle the default Leaflet icon marker rendering issues using custom Lucide icons within Leaflet `DivIcon`, for cleaner and visually appealing markers.


**Prioritizing a Direct Approach**

I'm now revising my approach to map integration due to potential compatibility issues. `react-leaflet` with its React 18 requirement and ESM concerns seems tricky in this environment, which led me to considering React 19 incompatibilities. I'll switch to using `leaflet` directly within a `useEffect` hook, referencing a `div` element. This keeps it robust and simple. I'll focus on getting a basic map with markers first, then explore more advanced features like the heatmap.


**Constructing the Map Component**

I'm now focused on the final code for the map itself. I've updated `index.html` to include Leaflet's CSS, and incorporated `leaflet` into the import map. I'm adding address fields to `types.ts`, and implementing a new form and map view using direct Leaflet integration in `Professional.tsx`. The geocoding service is also now implemented using Nominatim, with an improved query string. I will add the state report now.


**Refactoring Leaflet Integration**

I'm now changing my approach. I'm moving away from direct Leaflet and using `react-leaflet` to manage the map components. I'll need to rewrite the previous code to manage the Leaflet lifecycle within React components directly. I'm focusing on ensuring compatibility and a smooth integration with the existing React environment, handling initial load and updates. I'll also check if I need any additional type definitions for React Leaflet.

payload.text:
**Troubleshooting TypeScript Errors**

I'm currently focused on resolving a TypeScript error within `components/Professional.tsx`. The core issue revolves around arithmetic operations; the type system is complaining about the operands. I'm investigating the types involved to pinpoint where the type mismatch occurs and how to cast or restructure the code to satisfy TypeScript's requirements, specifically to enable arithmetic operations.


**Analyzing Type System Constraints**

I've homed in on the TypeScript error in `components/Professional.tsx`, specifically at line 658, within the leadsByState sorting operation. The error demands a 'number', 'bigint', or enum type for the comparison, and it relates to sorting the entries of `leadsByState`. Currently, I'm analyzing the `leadsByState` structure and its values to determine the actual types in that comparison. I need to ensure a suitable data type for effective arithmetic sorting.


**Clarifying Sorting Logic**

I'm now fully immersed in the sort operation within `components/Professional.tsx`, specifically focusing on line 658, where the `leadsByState` map entries are being sorted. The issue revolves around ensuring numeric comparison during the sorting of the `count` values. I am inspecting the `leadsByState` structure and how it is populated by the reduce operation, guaranteeing the proper numeric handling required for accurate descending order sorting by client count.


**Validating Number Types**

Currently, I'm verifying the number type consistency within the `leadsByState` map, specifically in the sort comparison at `components/Professional.tsx:658`. I've confirmed that the accumulation correctly produces a `Record<string, number>`, and that the `Object.entries` function returns `[string, number][]`, making the destructuring in the sort function `[, a]` and `[, b]` safe with numbers. I'm checking the broader context now, including the `leads` state and its possible influence on the number types. It does look to be an array of `Lead` type, so it shouldn't affect the number of values in the map.


**Debugging TypeScript Configuration**

I'm now investigating potential TypeScript version or configuration issues that might be causing the type mismatch in the sort function within `components/Professional.tsx`. I've confirmed that `Object.entries(leadsByState)` correctly returns `[string, number][]`, given the explicit type of `leadsByState` as `Record<string, number>`. However, the arithmetic operation within the sort function still throws an error. I'm exploring the project's `tsconfig.json` and its settings related to strict null checks, strict mode, and other configurations which might influence type inference in my environment. Perhaps an overlooked setting is enforcing a more restrictive type inference or something.


**Implementing Numeric Sorting**

I've streamlined the sorting logic in `components/Professional.tsx` to explicitly handle numeric comparisons. Replacing `.sort(([, a], [, b]) => b - a)` with `.sort((a, b) => b[1] - a[1])` ensures direct numeric subtraction on the second element of the tuple. The goal is to avoid any implicit type inference complications in favor of explicit `number` arithmetic, in the comparison on line 658.


**Resolving Numeric Comparison**

I've confirmed that the sorting issue at line 658 stems from the implicit type inference within the sort function. Replacing `.sort(([, a], [, b]) => b - a)` with `.sort((a, b) => b[1] - a[1])` should be more robust. The map iteration `Object.entries(leadsByState)` returns `[string, number][]`, making `a[1]` a `number`. This approach should avoid any unintended type coercion complications within the sorting implementation itself.


**Fixing Implicit Type Coercion**

I'm revisiting the sort operation in `components/Professional.tsx`, focusing on line 661 where the error flagged. The sort function attempts numeric subtraction `b - a`, but the type inference is likely causing issues. I'll test the updated version with `sort((a, b) => b[1] - a[1])` to directly access the `number` values in the entries.


**Verifying Type Inference**

I'm now revisiting the sorting logic in `components/Professional.tsx`. The recent change to `sort((a, b) => b[1] - a[1])` should be effective. I have confirmed that the type of `leadsByState` is `Record<string, number>`, and `Object.entries` produces `[string, number][]`. Thus, `a[1]` and `b[1]` are directly `number` types, which should resolve the error about arithmetic operations. I'll double-check for other related errors around line 658, but I believe this is the core issue.

The focus is to ensure the XML is well-formed, including the CDATA section for the code itself.

Aqui estão as alterações para garantir que todos os cartões de tarefas no Cronograma tenham exatamente as mesmas dimensões (altura e largura) e um layout interno rígido, independentemente do tamanho do texto inserido.

**Mudanças principais:**
1.  **Altura Fixa (`h-32`):** Mantida e reforçada para todos os cartões.
2.  **Largura Total (`w-full`):** Adicionada ao container pai para garantir que o cartão ocupe todo o espaço horizontal disponível.
3.  **Controle de Texto (`line-clamp-2`):** Substituí o `truncate` por `line-clamp-2`. Isso permite que títulos longos ocupem até duas linhas sem quebrar o layout, cortando o excesso elegantemente se passar disso.
4.  **Alinhamento Interno (`justify-between`):** O conteúdo interno do cartão agora usa `justify-between` com altura total (`h-full`). Isso força o título para o topo e as etiquetas (tempo, categoria) para a base do cartão, garantindo que visualmente todos os cartões pareçam idênticos, mesmo que um tenha pouco texto e o outro muito.

payload.text:
Quero que a primeira aba do assistente geral, do assistente pessoal, seja possível através dele registrar por áudios qualquer tarefa.
- Saldo positivo
- Saldo negativo
- Despesas
- Bonificações em dinheiro
- Recebi R$ 50 hoje.
- Recebi R$ 3.000 hoje.
- Tive um gasto de luz de R$ 150.
- Fui para a academia.
- Parei para almoçar.

Qualquer tarefa seja registrada e as tarefas sejam destinadas aos seus categorias corretas.
- Treinar na aba de treinar.
- Dormir na aba de dormir.
- Dinheiro na aba financeira.
- Prospecção de cliente na aba do CRM.

Que tudo isso reflita no dashboard e no relatório da última página também. Além de ser fixado, lógico, na sua aba correta. Então, o treino seja anexado na aba de treino, o dinheiro seja fixado na aba financeira e assim sucessivamente.

**Initiating The Process**

I'm currently breaking down the request to enable the "General Assistant." My focus is on understanding the types of commands it needs to handle: financial actions, tasks, workout instructions, and managing leads in the CRM. The next step will be mapping out how this "General Assistant" should interpret incoming commands.


**Mapping The Architecture**

I've been mapping the data stores needed for the assistant, focusing on how they interact. My thoughts have expanded to the specific inputs (voice/text commands) and desired outputs (tabs, dashboards, reports) for each module. The current constraint is that the Gemini AI service has to interpret natural language and then call the right function. I'm also assessing the existing code architecture. I'm especially focused on how the global state is handled.


**Refining Lead Handling**

I'm now zeroing in on lead management. The current setup in `Professional.tsx` presents a hurdle, so I plan to lift the CRM state to `App.tsx` for easier global updates. My plan involves adding an `addLead` tool to `geminiService.ts` and updating the `SYSTEM_INSTRUCTION` for CRM contexts. I'm also preparing to implement the new `addLead` command in the main handler within `App.tsx`. I'm also refining existing handlers.


**Enhancing The CRM Integration**

I'm now implementing the detailed code changes. I've added a new instruction to `geminiService.ts` to recognize CRM-related commands. I am including a new `addLeadTool` with parameters for lead details. In `App.tsx`, I'm moving mock data and managing leads and funnels with `useState`. I'm updating `handleCommand` to handle the `addLead` function, which creates and appends new leads. I'm also reviewing `logActivity` for category handling.


**Revising UI Interactions**

I'm now integrating the `addLead` function across various components. In `Professional.tsx`, I'm refactoring the component to receive props and removing local state management for leads and funnels. This will facilitate global state updates. I also realized the need to default the lead status to the first stage when it is not specified. The suggestion cards in `Dashboard.tsx` are okay. I'm focusing on handling new lead interactions in the backend service.


**Defining New Commands**

I'm now focusing on specific command mappings based on user input. I'm prioritizing `logWorkout` for workout descriptions and `logActivity` for time tracking. I've also mapped "Prospecção de cliente" to `addLead`. I am drafting code structures in `App.tsx`, `Professional.tsx`, and `geminiService.ts` as well. I'm making sure transaction details are handled using the existing `addTransaction` tool.


**Implementing New Commands**

I'm now integrating the additional commands into the existing framework. "Fui para a academia" translates directly to the `logWorkout` function, and "Parei para almoçar" maps neatly to the `logActivity` function. "Prospecção de cliente" is now explicitly handled by the `addLead` command. I am working on the related code updates, in all the relevant files. I'm focusing on ensuring all states are correctly handled.


**Updating Report Dataflow**

I'm now integrating the `leads` data into the `Reports.tsx` component. Since it already handles `tasks` and `transactions`, adding `leads` will enable CRM-related KPIs or logic to be displayed, though this is not specifically required. I'll modify the existing component to receive and use the `leads` data. This is my priority now.


**Reflecting on Current Data**

Now I'm making sure that the current state of leads updates the dashboard. I've realized the need for the global `leads` state to accurately reflect in `Dashboard.tsx` KPIs. This will naturally happen when updating the state in `App.tsx`. I am also now preparing for the XML generation. I'll need to be careful with `Professional.tsx`, and pass down the `leads` and `setLeads` props. I am following these action plans: in `geminiService.ts` I'm adding `addLead`, then moving state to `App.tsx`, and then adjusting Professional.tsx to work with props.

Para atender à sua solicitação, precisamos fazer com que o **Comando Central (Dashboard)** consiga manipular os dados de todas as outras abas, especificamente a aba de CRM (Profissional), que antes tinha seus dados isolados.

Aqui está o plano de execução:
1.  **Centralizar os Dados (`App.tsx`):** Vou mover o estado dos *Leads* (Clientes) e *Funis* de dentro do componente `Professional` para o `App` principal. Assim, o Assistente de IA pode adicionar um cliente novo e isso aparecerá instantaneamente na aba CRM.
2.  **Ensinar a IA (`geminiService.ts`):** Vou adicionar uma nova ferramenta (`tool`) chamada `addLead` para que a IA entenda quando você disser "Novo cliente...", "Prospecção...", etc.
3.  **Conectar as Pontas (`Professional.tsx`):** O componente de CRM deixará de usar dados locais e passará a receber os dados globais do aplicativo.

Aqui estão as alterações:

payload.text: Agora, na última aba, a aba de relatórios, a primeira coisa que deve aparecer nesta aba de relatórios é: eu quero que sejam as minhas metas. Essas metas vão ser vinculadas a partir do que for escrito na primeira aba, no assistente geral. O assistente geral, a principal função dele vai ser entender quais são as metas e conseguir ajudar qual será o procedimento, qual será o caminho para alcançar essas metas. E eu quero que o resultado delas, a progressão delas, fique explícito em relatórios, na última aba.

payload.text:
- Para um controlador geral, preciso que o microfone funcione.
- Preciso que a pessoa, nosso cliente no caso, consiga clicar no microfone, fale, e a própria inteligência artificial entenda o que o cliente está dizendo.
- Isso funcionará de forma automática, sem que o cliente precise ficar escrevendo.

**Initiating Voice Integration**

I'm currently analyzing the request to enable voice interaction for the General Controller. My focus is on understanding the core functionality: specifically how a user will initiate voice input, speak their commands, and what data the system will need to extract and act upon. I am breaking down the user experience.


**Evaluating Speech Implementation**

I've examined the initial attempt at voice integration in `Dashboard.tsx`. I see the `webkitSpeechRecognition` logic with a mic button, but the user wants it fully *automatic*. That implies enhancements are needed, perhaps auto-submission after speaking. I also need to ensure broad browser compatibility since Web Speech API's support varies, especially with Firefox.


**Refining Speech Handling**

I'm now zeroing in on the speech recognition logic itself. My plan involves immediate text capture and integration with `handleSend` to eliminate manual submission. I also need to make sure 'pt-BR' is the language. I'll need to figure out automatic submission based on a pause in speaking. I need to make sure the user gets feedback that the system is listening.


**Implementing Microphone Access**

I'm now focused on enabling the microphone and improving the user experience. I've added `"microphone"` to `requestFramePermissions` in `metadata.json`, and I'm refactoring the `toggleListening` function. The UI update involves visual feedback for when the mic is active; I want the microphone button to clearly signal when it's listening, like the initial use of `animate-pulse` but enhanced. I need the microphone to stop listening when clicked again and integrate the speech transcript directly to `handleSend` to implement auto submission.


**Automating Command Submission**

I've determined that automatic submission is the right approach. Now I am refining the `toggleListening` function to handle the `recognition` instance to allow canceling when clicked again. I am updating `handleSend` to accept the captured text for auto-submission. I am now proceeding to make the code changes for `metadata.json` and the updated `toggleListening` function.


**Adjusting Recognition Logic**

I'm currently working to refine the voice recognition flow. I've integrated `recognitionRef.current?.stop()` within `toggleListening` to ensure a clean stop when the microphone is toggled off. I've also implemented the automatic submission of the transcript by calling `handleSend` in `onresult`. I'm now making sure the browser compatibility alert shows if the feature is not supported. I am also making sure that only one sentence is recognized.


**Finalizing Error Handling**

I'm now integrating error handling and final adjustments in `Dashboard.tsx`. I'm focusing on managing the `isListening` state within the `onerror` and `onend` event handlers of the speech recognition to prevent unexpected behavior. I'm also confirming the visual cue ("Ouvindo sua voz...") remains to provide feedback to the user and preparing to confirm the successful integration with the broader system.


**Formulating XML Integration**

I'm now evaluating how to incorporate the XML structure into the existing workflow. I will be assessing how to best format and integrate the XML structure into the command submission process for optimal compatibility with WithNexus (Gemini). I will examine the most efficient methods for this integration.

Para garantir que o microfone funcione como um verdadeiro "Controlador por Voz", onde o usuário fala e a IA processa imediatamente sem precisar digitar ou clicar em enviar, farei as seguintes alterações:

1.  **Atualização de Permissões (`metadata.json`)**: Habilitar a permissão de uso do microfone.

Implementei um sistema de **Autenticação Simulada com Google** e vinculei todos os dados ao perfil do usuário.

**O que foi feito:**

1.  **Nova Tela de Login:** Substituí o formulário simples por uma tela profissional com o botão **"Entrar com Google"**.
2.  **Simulação de Auth:** Ao clicar, o sistema simula uma conexão segura e cria um perfil de usuário "Google" (com foto e e-mail fictícios).
3.  **Vínculo de Dados (Data Binding):**
    *   Refatorei a arquitetura para que **nenhum dado seja carregado antes do login**.
    *   Todos os dados (Tarefas, Finanças, CRM) agora são salvos com uma "chave" única ligada ao ID do usuário (`nexus_USERID_transactions`, etc).
    *   Isso garante que o que você pediu ("todos os dados registrados e vinculados a uma conta") seja atendido tecnicamente.

**Como testar:**
1.  Aguarde o reload do app.
2.  Clique em **"Entrar com Google"**.
3.  O sistema criará seu perfil e liberará o acesso ao Nexus 360.
4.  Todos os dados que você criar agora estarão salvos especificamente para esta "conta".

*Nota: Como este é um ambiente de demonstração sem servidor backend, a autenticação é uma simulação frontend de alta fidelidade para demonstrar o fluxo de experiência do usuário.*

payload.text:
- Create a memory system to record everything the client requested.
- When the client requests to schedule a task, the task should only be considered complete if the client states that it has already been performed.
- If the client asks to schedule a task without mentioning completion, such as scheduling a meeting for 8:00 PM, the task should remain incomplete.

**Considering Memory Systems**

I'm exploring memory systems to track client requests and enhance task status management. I'm currently using `localStorage` for data persistence with a custom `useStickyState` hook in `App.tsx`. I need to assess how well this supports more complex client data and future scalability.


**Analyzing Task Lifecycle**

I'm focused on the task lifecycle. My system needs to correctly manage task completion. It's imperative that I don't set tasks to "complete" automatically, rather, completion must come from explicit client confirmation. I need to make some code adjustments in `handleCommand` to reflect this change.


**Integrating Completion Status**

I'm updating the `geminiService.ts` to include a `isCompleted` parameter in `logActivityTool`. This will allow the AI to accurately log past or future activities. In `App.tsx`, I'll modify `handleCommand` to account for this new parameter when a task is created. I also need to make sure the chat history is persistent across navigations.


**Implementing Persistent Chat History**

I'm now focused on persistent chat history across tab changes and reloads. I've decided to move the `messages` state from `Dashboard` to `App.tsx` using `useStickyState`. This seems the best way to ensure the chat history is retained. I also need to update `handleCommand` in `App.tsx` to handle the `isCompleted` parameter from `geminiService.ts`. I anticipate needing to make changes to `types.ts`, but this should be minimal.


**Revising Completion Logic**

I'm adjusting the completion logic now. I'm updating `components/Dashboard.tsx` to receive `messages` and `setMessages` as props, removing the local state. I'm also refining the system instruction in `geminiService.ts` to be explicit about setting `isCompleted`. I'm updating `logActivityTool` parameters and adding `messages` persistence in `App.tsx`, and I will ensure that the changes for completion status are applied within `handleCommand`. Finally, I've exported the `Message` interface from `types.ts`.


**Adding Completion Indicator**

I'm now integrating the `isCompleted` parameter into the `logActivityTool` within `geminiService.ts` to explicitly track task completion. I'm also updating `App.tsx` to pass the `messages` and `setMessages` state to the `Dashboard` component, ensuring consistent chat history management.


**Clarifying Task Instructions**

I'm refining my understanding of the new system instructions for task completion. I'm focusing on the condition where a task is only deemed complete if the client explicitly confirms its completion. I will update `geminiService.ts` to include this directive in the `SYSTEM_INSTRUCTION`. I'm going to update `Dashboard.tsx` to handle passing and handling the state. I think it is the best method.


**Coding Gemini Integration**

My current focus is writing the code for the Gemini integration. I'm updating `geminiService.ts` to include the `logActivityTool` and the `SYSTEM_INSTRUCTION`. In `App.tsx`, I'm adding `messages` with `useStickyState` and updating `handleCommand`. Finally, I'm updating `Dashboard.tsx` to handle the `messages` and `setMessages` props.

To implement the memory system and the smart task scheduling logic (distinguishing between future schedules and completed activities), I will update the app to persist chat history and refine the AI's instructions.

Here are the changes:

1.  **`services/geminiService.ts`**:
    *   Updated the **System Instruction** to explicitly teach the AI to distinguish between "scheduling/planning" (incomplete) and "logging/reporting" (completed).
    *   Updated the `logActivityTool` definition to include an `isCompleted` boolean parameter.

2.  **`App.tsx`**:
    *   Added `messages` to the persistent state (Memory System), linked to the user's profile. Now the chat history is saved even if you close the browser.
    *   Updated the `handleCommand` logic. Now, when `logActivity` is called, it checks `args.isCompleted`.
        *   If `true` (e.g., "I studied"): Marks as done and gives XP immediately.
        *   If `false` (e.g., "Schedule meeting for 8pm"): Creates the task as pending (unchecked) and does not give XP yet.

3.  **`components/Dashboard.tsx`**:
    *   Removed local state for messages and now receives them from `App.tsx` to ensure persistence.

entries.description: Update AI instructions and tools to distinguish between completed logs and future schedules.
Add chat persistence (memory system) and update task completion logic.
Remove local state messages and accept persisted messages as props.

payload.text:
- Create a professional, stylized, and futuristic logo for the name "Nexus".
- The "N" should be the primary characteristic of this logo.
- I want a written logo.
- You can use colors similar to those we are already using.
- This logo will be located primarily in the login area, appearing above the company name and the logo itself.

**Defining the 'N'**

I've started by breaking down the request. I'm focusing on "Nexus," and the directive to use "N" as the central element in the logo. **Analyzing Design Concepts**

I'm now iterating on the logo design, aiming for a visual that resonates. I've considered several concepts: a circuit-like 'N,' which might be too intricate, and an infinity-loop 'N,' which felt a bit gentle. My current frontrunner is a sharp, geometric design: an 'N' built from two vertical lines and a diagonal slash, which I believe will embody the futuristic and professional tone.


**Constructing a Digital Handshake**

I'm now refining the "N" shape to embody connection and futurism. I'm exploring a split diagonal line, aiming for a digital handshake aesthetic, visualized through SVG paths. My current focus is on a dual-path approach with a gap, reflecting data flow. I also considered a hexagon frame to provide a bolder look.


**Refining the Visualization**

I'm now implementing a geometric "N" comprised of disconnected vertical bars and a floating diagonal. My goal is a modern aesthetic, ditching the container for a bold look. I'm focusing on a gradient stroke for the diagonal and will explore drop shadow/glow effects to add visual polish. This is a very interesting concept, so I'm excited to see how it comes together. I am now looking to add the logo into the code.


**Implementing the Logo**

I'm now integrating the SVG logo into the application. I'm focusing on creating a reusable `NexusLogo` component to maintain visual consistency across the login screen and the sidebar. The goal is to replace the existing "N" text with this new, defined SVG. The current iteration uses two vertical bars and a digital diagonal for a futuristic "N".


**Defining Color Application**

I'm now focusing on integrating the specified colors into the logo. I'm using a gradient definition within the SVG to achieve the requested effect. I'm working with the path data for the "N" and will incorporate circle nodes at the diagonal's endpoints for added visual interest. The objective is to apply a gradient for an elegant look. I've also formulated an implementation plan.

I've also refined my code. I am working on how to incorporate the changes into the app.


**Enhancing the Aesthetic**

I'm now revising the code with the design in mind. I've broken the vertical lines subtly for a more modern look, and I'm planning to use a `drop-shadow` effect via CSS to create a glowing effect, adding further visual appeal. The plan is to create different implementations on the login screen, sidebar, and dashboard header to incorporate the changes. I'm now working to create the XML.


**Revising Code Architecture**

I'm now focusing on a plan to incorporate the updated design. I've decided to prioritize the login area, as the prompt directed, while ensuring visual consistency throughout the app. My plan involves updating `App.tsx` (Login + Sidebar) and `Dashboard.tsx` (Header). The "N" will be a gradient-filled, sleek, high-tech design.


**Integrating the Design**

I'm now focusing on integrating the icon and text elements for the login screen. I'll be implementing the design in `App.tsx` and `Dashboard.tsx`, ensuring the logo icon is prominently displayed alongside the "NEXUS" text and a "360" tagline. I'm aiming for a visually balanced presentation that aligns with the professional and futuristic aesthetic. I've also formulated an implementation plan. I'll provide the full content of the updated files for review.

To give the application a world-class, futuristic identity, I have designed a custom SVG logo.

**Design Specification:**
1.  **The "N" Mark:** I replaced the generic square "N" with a custom vector graphic. It features two vertical pillars representing stability (foundation), connected by a dynamic diagonal gradient representing flow and transformation. The edges are rounded for a modern feel, but the structure is geometric.
2.  **Color Palette:** I utilized the existing `Blue-500` to `Purple-600` gradients but applied them directly to the vector paths for a "glowing" neon effect against the dark background.
3.  **Typography:** The "NEXUS" text is now styled with `tracking-widest` (letter spacing) to give it a cinematic, sci-fi title look.
4.  **Placement:**
    *   **Login Screen:** The logo is the hero element, large and centered, with a distinct visual hierarchy.
    *   **Sidebar & Dashboard:** The logo is scaled down but retains its geometry, ensuring brand consistency across the app.

Here are the changes:

Update Dashboard Header to use new Nexus Logo Branding.

It seems unlikely, but it's worth exploring the impact of viewport changes during map initialization. The removal of the view toggles alters the width of the container, which combined with the `justify-between` property causes an appearance of shrinkage, though the buttons themselves likely are not changing size. I've focused on adding `whitespace-nowrap` to prevent text wrapping, and I'll make the buttons `shrink-0` to ensure their width remains constant within the flex container. **Investigating Flex Layout**

I'm now diving deeper into the flex layout, specifically how Leaflet's CSS and the map container's styling might be influencing button sizing. I'm focusing on the `flex gap-2` container and its items, particularly with `whitespace-nowrap` now applied. It seems the "view toggles" have different sizing, which impacts container height, so I'm investigating those styles, especially `p-1`, to ensure consistency across the buttons. The goal is to isolate any unexpected effects on button dimensions.


**Optimizing Button Consistency**

The div is roughly 34px tall, while other buttons are about 32px, and this difference might be causing the visual "jump" when the toggles are removed. To stabilize the UI, I'll remove the conditional rendering, keeping the view toggles visible, and making them functional in map mode. This should provide consistent height and enable direct switching, eliminating the appearance of resizing.


**Reframing View Options**

I'm now integrating the map toggle into the view options. My goal is to make the map button part of the view options by merging it. I'm focusing on consistency. I'll remove the conditional rendering for the view toggles to simplify the layout. All of this should fix the visual shrinking.


**Standardizing Button Heights**

I will standardize padding and apply a consistent height class (`h-9` or `h-10`) to all buttons, along with `flex items-center`. I plan to integrate the map button into the view toggle group for unified control. Removing the conditional rendering of the view toggles group will also prevent the layout jumps.

I plan to make the Kanban and List buttons active or inactive based on the `viewMode`. I'll make the map button a toggle, integrating it smoothly, and I plan to keep the view toggles always visible. I'm evaluating the positioning of the "Audit," "New Client," and "Map" action buttons in relation to these elements, ensuring efficient user access.

A potential issue is that if the input string format changes slightly, or Tailwind's JIT compiler doesn't recognize the constructed classes, it might lead to broken styling. This may be the source of the user's issue with a "poorly formed border". I'm considering mapping colors instead, or ensuring `stage.color` (from `types.ts` and `constants.ts`) is used more directly, for a more robust approach.
